# Discussion

This study analyzed 6,833 emails from the operational correspondence of the CITF Databank to characterize how large-scale health research data sharing is coordinated in practice.
Three principal findings emerged.
First, governance and data operations workstreams ran largely in parallel, with limited overlap except at the point of document review and reconciliation.
Second, contractual and administrative concerns persisted throughout the entire data sharing lifecycle rather than being resolved in early phases and set aside.
Third, coordination labour was highly concentrated among a small number of Databank personnel, with systematic differences in workload distribution across institutional roles and gender.

These findings have direct implications for how research data sharing infrastructure is resourced and governed in Canada and beyond.
The 18:1 ratio of administrative to technical time, with studies spending a median of 395 days in DSA negotiation and 398 days in data preparation, compared to only 22 days for technical cataloguing, suggests that growing data sharing obligations under the Tri-Agency RDM Policy [@triagency2021] will place increasing demands on university contracts offices and research administration teams.
Demands are not easily scaled: contracts office capacity is typically constrained by fixed institutional overhead rates rather than by the volume of agreements requiring processing.
Policymakers and research administrators should account for this bottleneck when designing governance frameworks and allocating human resources for data sharing initiatives.

The structural separation between governance and data operations workstreams, and the finding that contractual and administrative concerns persist across all phases rather than being resolved early, suggests that current models of sequential data sharing, in which agreements are finalized before technical work begins, may not reflect operational realities.
Parallel onboarding strategies, in which technical preparation and agreement negotiation proceed simultaneously, could reduce overall timelines and alleviate pressure on the DSA negotiation phase, which was the single largest source of delay in this dataset.
More responsive and adaptive models for data sharing agreements, which can be amended as data requirements evolve rather than requiring full renegotiation, may also reduce the persistence of governance concerns in later phases [@casey2016; @vanhuis2014].

The concentration of email activity within Databank teams, with the Harmonization Group sending at a ratio of 116 emails per sender and the Secretariat at 101 emails per sender, compared to 5--7 among partner study representatives, makes visible the coordination labour that is often unrecognized in funding and workload planning.
Sustaining data sharing at scale requires recognizing and resourcing this labour explicitly.
The gender analysis adds a further dimension to this concern: Data Operations topics were the most male-dominated, while Study Documentation topics showed the highest representation of women, suggesting that the more visible, technically-oriented work was disproportionately associated with men while women were more concentrated in documentation and cataloguing tasks.
This pattern is consistent with prior research on the feminization of hidden labour in research data infrastructure [@choroszewicz2022].
Future initiatives should consider how coordination roles are defined, compensated, and credited, and whether existing structures inadvertently concentrate invisible labour among women and other underrepresented groups.

This study has several limitations.
The corpus is drawn from a single coordinator's inbox, which captures one perspective on the coordination process but may not reflect the full scope of communication across the initiative.
Emails from other team members, including the Databank's scientific director and data analysts, were not included in this analysis but will be incorporated in future work [@chiefscience2025[This reference seems out of place]{.mark}].
The gender analysis relies on probabilistic coding of first names, which does not capture non-binary or gender-diverse identities and may misclassify names from non-Western cultural contexts.
Finally, as a single case study, the findings reflect the specific organizational and institutional context of the CITF Databank; while we believe the structural patterns identified here are likely generalizable to similar pan-Canadian data sharing initiatives, further research across other settings is needed to confirm this.

Despite these limitations, this study makes a contribution to the empirical understanding of health data sharing governance.
By analyzing operational correspondence as it actually unfolded, rather than relying on retrospective surveys or post-hoc reflections, we are able to document the day-to-day realities of coordinating data sharing at scale in a way that prior research has not.
The use of topic modeling applied to email archives offers a replicable methodological approach that could be adapted to study coordination challenges in other domains of health systems management, including interjurisdictional care delivery, health human resource coordination, and the governance of emerging digital health platforms [@lossioventura2021; @indig2023].

As Canada develops its national scientific data ecosystem and institutions build capacity to meet Tri-Agency RDM obligations, evidence-based guidance on the organizational and human resource requirements of data sharing is urgently needed.
This study provides a first empirical look at what that work actually involves, and points toward the governance reforms and resource commitments that will be required to sustain it.
